payload.formula_text:
Open Rate = (Unique Opens / Unique Recipients) × 100
Click Rate = (Unique Clicks / Unique Recipients) × 100
payload.formula_explanation: Compares engagement metrics between manually-sent campaigns and automated flow emails. Flows typically show higher engagement because they are triggered by specific user actions or behaviors, making them more relevant and timely.
how_to_read: Blue bars show open rate for each email source type
Green bars show click rate for each source type
Compare bar heights to see which source drives better engagement
Flows often outperform campaigns due to behavioral targeting
Large gaps between campaign and flow performance suggest optimization opportunities
decisions_answered: Which email type drives more engagement - campaigns or flows?
Should we invest more in building automated flows?
Are our manual campaigns performing as well as automated emails?
Where should we focus optimization efforts for maximum impact?
payload.narration_text:
This bar chart compares engagement between your manual campaigns and automated flows.

Blue bars show open rates, green bars show click rates. Compare the heights to see which performs better.

Flows typically outperform campaigns because they're triggered by user behavior - abandoned carts, browse activity, post-purchase. This makes them more relevant and timely.

If you see large gaps between campaign and flow performance, it suggests you should invest more in building automated flows. If campaigns are lagging significantly, focus optimization there first.

Use this to decide: Where should you invest development time? Are campaigns worth the manual effort?

payload.formula_text:
Open Rate = (Unique Opens / Unique Recipients) × 100
Click Rate = (Unique Clicks / Unique Recipients) × 100
payload.formula_explanation: Compares engagement rates across different campaign content categories. Content types are derived from campaign names - promotional, product launch, newsletter, and other. This reveals which content resonates most with your audience.
how_to_read: Blue bars show open rate for each content category
Purple bars show click rate for each category
Higher bars indicate stronger engagement for that content type
Compare open vs click rates to understand engagement depth
Content types with high opens but low clicks may need better CTAs
decisions_answered: Which content type drives the most engagement?
Should we send more promotional or newsletter content?
Are product launches generating sufficient interest?
Which content types need optimization to improve clicks?
payload.narration_text:
This horizontal bar chart compares engagement across different content categories.

Blue bars represent open rates, purple bars represent click rates. Content types are derived from campaign names - promotional, product launch, newsletter, and other.

Look at both metrics together. High opens with low clicks means people are opening but not engaging - you might need better calls to action. Low opens suggest subject line or timing issues.

Typically, promotional content has higher clicks because it has clear actions. Newsletters build relationships but may have lower immediate engagement.

Use this to decide: What content should you create more of? What needs optimization?

payload.formula_explanation: Measures the health of your email list by showing the breakdown of subscribed, unsubscribed, and never-subscribed profiles. A healthy list has high subscription rates and low unsubscribe counts, indicating good list hygiene and audience quality.
how_to_read: Green segment shows actively subscribed profiles who can receive marketing emails
Red segment shows profiles who have unsubscribed from marketing
Gray segment shows profiles who were never subscribed (e.g., transactional only)
Center value displays total profiles in your database
Health indicator badge shows overall list health status
decisions_answered: What percentage of our list is actively subscribed?
Do we have a concerning unsubscribe rate?
How many profiles could we potentially convert to subscribers?
Is our list health improving or declining over time?
payload.narration_text:
This gauge chart shows the health of your email subscriber list.

Green represents actively subscribed profiles - these can receive your marketing emails. Red shows unsubscribed profiles who opted out. Gray shows profiles who never subscribed, often from transactional interactions.

The center displays your total profile count. The health badge at top right indicates overall status - green for healthy, yellow for caution, red for concern.

Above 90% subscribed is excellent. Between 70 and 90% is acceptable but worth monitoring. Below 70% indicates list health issues.

Use this to answer: Is our list healthy? Do we have unsubscribe problems? How many profiles could we convert to subscribers?

payload.formula_text:
Open Rate = (Unique Opens / Unique Recipients) × 100
Click Rate = (Unique Clicks / Unique Recipients) × 100
payload.formula_explanation: This chart tracks three key metrics over 90 days: total emails sent (volume), open rate (engagement), and click rate (action). Open and click rates are calculated using unique recipients to avoid inflation from multiple opens or clicks by the same person.
how_to_read: Blue area shows total email volume sent per week (scaled to thousands)
Green line tracks open rate percentage - aim for 25%+ for healthy engagement
Purple line tracks click rate percentage - aim for 3%+ for strong performance
Rising trends indicate improving engagement; declining trends warrant investigation
Look for correlation between volume changes and engagement rate changes
decisions_answered: Is our overall email engagement improving or declining over time?
Does sending more emails hurt our engagement rates?
When did significant performance shifts occur that need investigation?
Are we maintaining healthy open and click rates consistently?
payload.narration_text:
This chart shows your email program performance over the past 90 days with three key metrics.

The blue area represents total emails sent each week, scaled to thousands. The green line tracks your open rate - aim for 25% or higher for healthy engagement. The purple line shows click rate - 3% or higher indicates strong performance.

Watch for trends: Rising lines mean improving engagement. If you see declining trends, investigate what changed. Also look for correlation - sometimes sending more emails can hurt engagement rates.

This chart helps you answer: Is engagement improving? Does volume affect engagement? And when did major shifts occur that need attention?

payload.formula_text: Source Share = (Source Emails Sent / Total Emails Sent) × 100
payload.formula_explanation: Shows the split of email volume between campaigns and flows. The center value displays total emails sent across all sources. This helps understand the balance between proactive (campaign) and reactive (flow) email marketing.
how_to_read: Each slice represents the percentage of total emails from that source
Center value shows total emails sent in the period
Hover over slices to see exact counts and percentages
A healthy mix often has 60-70% flows and 30-40% campaigns
Heavy campaign skew may indicate under-developed automation
decisions_answered: What is the balance between campaigns and automated flows?
Are we over-relying on manual campaigns?
Do we have enough automated email infrastructure?
How many total emails are we sending to customers?
payload.narration_text:
This donut chart shows how your email volume splits between campaigns and flows.

Each slice represents the percentage from that source. The center shows your total emails sent. Hover for exact numbers.

A healthy email program typically has 60 to 70 percent of volume from flows and 30 to 40 percent from campaigns. Flows are automated and scale without manual effort.

If you see heavy campaign volume, it may indicate under-developed automation. This is a common growth area - building more flows reduces manual work while often improving engagement.

Use this to assess: Are you relying too much on manual campaigns? Is your automation infrastructure mature enough?

payload.formula_text: Monthly Revenue = Sum of all order values attributed to Klaviyo in that month
payload.formula_explanation: Tracks total order value attributed to email marketing through Klaviyo over the past 6 months. This includes revenue from both campaign and flow-attributed conversions, showing the direct financial impact of your email program.
how_to_read: Area height shows total order revenue for each month (scaled to thousands)
Rising trend indicates growing email-attributed revenue
Summary values at top right show total revenue and order count
Seasonal patterns may appear - compare year-over-year when possible
Sudden drops warrant investigation into email performance or attribution
decisions_answered: How much revenue is email marketing generating?
Is email revenue growing or declining over time?
What are the seasonal patterns in email-attributed orders?
How does email contribute to overall business revenue?
payload.narration_text:
This area chart tracks revenue attributed to your email marketing over the past 6 months.

The height of the gold area shows total order value each month, scaled to thousands. The summary at top right shows cumulative totals - total revenue and order count.

A rising trend indicates your email program is generating more revenue over time. If you see decline, investigate what changed - email performance, attribution windows, or business factors.

Watch for seasonal patterns. Holiday months typically spike. Compare year-over-year when possible to separate growth from seasonality.

This answers the critical question: How much is email actually worth to your business?
